coverage_notes: Introduces atoms as the specific particles that make up elements. Establishes that different atoms = different elements. Bridges particles_idea (everything is particles) to atomic_structure (what's inside an atom).

rendering_guide.section: Headed paragraph block with spacing above/below
rendering_guide.callout: Highlighted box. Styles: definition, key_idea, fun_fact, remember, mind_blow
rendering_guide.list: Styled list. Styles: icon_list, two_column, comparison, challenge, numbered_steps
rendering_guide.transition: Italic bridge text, lighter font weight

student_flow.step_1: READ Part 1 (hook — if everything is particles, why is gold different from iron?)
student_flow.step_2: READ Part 2 (core — what atoms are, elements, atomic number)
student_flow.step_4: WATCH matched video (atom intro, element diversity)
student_flow.step_5: READ Part 3 (scale — how small atoms really are)
student_flow.step_7: READ Part 4 (atoms combine into everything — bridge to atomic_structure)

parts.part_title: If Everything Is Particles, Why Is Gold Different From Iron?
parts.purpose: Hook — pose the question that atoms answer

blocks.body: We now know everything is made of tiny particles in constant motion. But that raises a problem. If a gold bar and an iron nail are both made of particles, why are they so different? Gold is yellow, shiny, and never rusts. Iron is grey, strong, but leave it in the rain and it crumbles into orange powder.

If everything is just 'particles,' what makes one substance different from another? The answer: not all particles are the same. There are different TYPES of particles — and each type creates a different substance.

These fundamental types of particles have a name: atoms. And the story of atoms is the story of how 118 different building blocks create everything in the universe — from stars to smartphones, from blood to diamonds.

blocks.title: Hold Two Metals
blocks.body: Pick up two different metal objects if you can — a gold ring and a silver spoon, or a copper coin and a steel key. Anything metallic and different from each other.

Hold one in each hand. Feel the difference. One might be heavier, colder to the touch, or a different color. Both are shiny. Both are hard. Both conduct heat from your hand quickly. They clearly share some properties — they're both metals. But they're obviously not the same substance.

Both are made of particles. Both particles are packed in a tight metal grid. So why the difference? Because the particles themselves are fundamentally different. A gold particle is not the same as an iron particle. Each type of particle is a different kind of atom.

Okay, so we've established that everything is tiny particles bouncing around. Cool. But here's the obvious follow-up question: if my phone and a potato are both 'tiny particles,' why can I make calls on one but not the other?

Saying 'everything is particles' is like saying 'every building is made of bricks.' Technically true, but a hospital and a prison are VERY different buildings. The type of brick matters. The arrangement matters.

The chemistry version of different 'bricks' are called atoms. There are about 118 types. And those 118 types, mixed and matched in different combinations, create literally EVERYTHING. Every substance, every material, every weird thing you've ever touched. 118 types of LEGO bricks → infinite builds.

parts.purpose: Core teaching — what atoms are, what elements are, atomic number, the periodic table preview

blocks.title: Atom
blocks.body: The smallest unit of an element that still has the properties of that element. An atom of gold behaves like gold. An atom of iron behaves like iron. Break it further and it stops being that element.

blocks.title: One Type of Atom = One Element
blocks.body: Each of the 118 known elements is defined by its type of atom. Hydrogen atoms are the simplest and lightest — one of everything inside. Carbon atoms are what make life possible — they form the backbone of DNA, proteins, and sugars. Oxygen atoms are what you're breathing right now. Iron atoms make your blood red. Gold atoms resist reacting with almost anything, which is why gold jewelry from ancient Egypt still gleams thousands of years later.

blocks.title: What Makes Each Atom Different?
blocks.body: Every atom has a core called the nucleus containing tiny particles called protons. The number of protons defines which element an atom is. 1 proton = hydrogen. 6 protons = carbon. 26 protons = iron. 79 protons = gold. This number is called the atomic number — it's an atom's identity card.

blocks.title: The Periodic Table: The Menu of Atoms
blocks.body: All 118 elements are organized on the periodic table — a chart that arranges atoms by their atomic number and groups elements with similar behavior together. You don't need to memorize it yet. Just know it exists, and it's the single most useful cheat sheet in all of science. Every element has a symbol: H for hydrogen, C for carbon, O for oxygen, Fe for iron, Au for gold.

blocks.description: Simple periodic table highlighting a few familiar elements: H, C, O, Fe, Au, Na, Cl — with their atomic numbers visible

blocks.title: Atoms Combine to Make Compounds
blocks.body: Most things around you aren't pure elements — they're compounds: different types of atoms bonded together. Water is hydrogen atoms bonded to oxygen atoms (H₂O). Table salt is sodium atoms bonded to chlorine atoms (NaCl). The magic of compounds is that they have completely different properties from the atoms that make them up. Two dangerous elements — explosive sodium and toxic chlorine — combine to make something you sprinkle on your dinner.

blocks.title: Element vs Compound
blocks.body: Element = one type of atom (gold, oxygen, carbon). Compound = two or more types of atoms chemically bonded (water, salt, sugar). The properties of a compound are different from its component elements.

blocks.title: See the Difference With Your Eyes
blocks.body: Look at a piece of copper — a penny, a wire, a pipe fitting. That warm reddish color? Every atom in there is a copper atom, with exactly 29 protons in its nucleus. No exceptions. That's what makes it copper.

Now look at something silver-colored — a stainless steel fork, aluminum foil, a nickel coin. Different color, different weight, different feel. Different atoms. Aluminum has 13 protons. Nickel has 28. Iron (in stainless steel) has 26. The proton count changes EVERYTHING about how the atom behaves.

blocks.title: The Identity Rule
blocks.body: Change even one proton and you have a completely different element. 79 protons = gold. Remove one proton and you'd have 78 protons = platinum. Add one and you'd have 80 protons = mercury. The difference between a solid yellow metal, a solid silvery metal, and a liquid metal at room temperature — just one proton.

blocks.title: Now Combine Them
blocks.body: Look at the water in a glass. Every molecule contains two hydrogen atoms (1 proton each) bonded to one oxygen atom (8 protons). None of them behave like hydrogen gas (explosive) or oxygen gas (feeds fire) anymore. Bonded together as H₂O, they create a completely new substance — the liquid that makes life possible.

Grab some table salt. Each tiny crystal contains sodium atoms (11 protons) locked in alternation with chlorine atoms (17 protons). Sodium alone explodes in water. Chlorine alone is a poisonous yellow gas. Together as NaCl? A harmless seasoning. Same atoms, but bonding transforms them completely.

blocks.title: Atom
blocks.body: The smallest piece of an element that still behaves like that element. Think of it as the minimum viable product of matter. Any smaller and it loses its identity.

blocks.title: 118 Flavors of Atom
blocks.body: There are about 118 different types of atoms, and each type is a different element. Hydrogen is the simplest — it's the plain vanilla of the atom world. Carbon is the networking king — bonds with everything and makes life possible. Gold is the diva — 79 protons, doesn't rust, doesn't react, looks amazing doing nothing. Iron is the blue-collar worker — strong, useful, but leave it in rain and it throws a rust tantrum.

blocks.title: What Makes Them Different? Proton Count.
blocks.body: Every atom has a core (the nucleus) containing protons. The number of protons IS the element. 1 proton? That's hydrogen. 6 protons? Carbon. 79 protons? Gold. This number is called the atomic number and it's basically an atom's social security number. No two elements share the same one.
Change one proton and you literally become a different element. 79 protons = gold. 80 protons = mercury (a LIQUID metal). One proton is the difference between a treasure and a hazardous spill.

blocks.title: When Atoms Get Married
blocks.body: Most things aren't single elements — they're compounds: different atoms chemically bonded. Water = hydrogen + oxygen married together (H₂O). Salt = sodium + chlorine in a committed relationship (NaCl). And just like human marriages, the couple has a completely different personality than either partner alone. Sodium: explosive. Chlorine: literal chemical weapon. Sodium chloride: goes great on fries.

blocks.title: Quick Summary
blocks.body: Element = one type of atom = one proton count. Compound = different atoms bonded together = new substance with new properties. 118 elements → millions of possible compounds.

questions.question: What determines which element an atom is?

options.A: Its size — bigger atoms are heavier elements
options.B: Its color — each element has a unique color
options.C: Its number of protons — the atomic number is the element's identity
options.D: Its location — atoms become different elements in different environments

questions.explanation: The number of protons in the nucleus defines the element. 1 proton = hydrogen, 8 protons = oxygen, 79 protons = gold. Change the proton count and you change the element entirely. This is the atomic number.

questions.question: Hydrogen gas is explosive and oxygen gas feeds fire. But water (H₂O) puts OUT fire. Why are the compound's properties so different from its elements?

options.A: Water dilutes the dangerous properties of hydrogen and oxygen
options.B: When atoms bond into a compound, they create a new substance with entirely new properties
options.C: The hydrogen and oxygen cancel each other's dangers out mathematically
options.D: Water isn't actually made of hydrogen and oxygen — it's its own element

questions.explanation: Compounds are new substances with their own unique properties. The atoms in water are chemically bonded in a way that fundamentally changes how they behave. It's not a mix of hydrogen and oxygen — it's a completely different substance.

questions.question: Gold has 79 protons. If you could somehow remove one proton, what would happen?

options.B: You'd have damaged gold that would repair itself over time
options.C: You'd have platinum — a completely different element with 78 protons
options.D: Nothing — removing one proton wouldn't make a difference

questions.explanation: The proton count IS the element. 79 protons = gold. 78 protons = platinum. Changing even a single proton transforms one element into an entirely different one with different color, density, and chemical behavior.

parts.purpose: Build intuition for atomic scale — mind-blowing comparisons that make size tangible

blocks.title: Smaller Than You Can Imagine
blocks.body: We've been saying atoms are 'incredibly tiny,' but let's try to actually grasp how small that is. Because the scale is genuinely hard for the human brain to process.

items.label: The Hair Comparison
items.body: A human hair is about 100,000 nanometers wide. An atom is about 0.1 to 0.3 nanometers across. That means roughly a million atoms could line up across the width of a single hair.

items.label: The Drop of Water
items.body: A single drop of water contains approximately 1,670,000,000,000,000,000,000 (1.67 sextillion) water molecules. Each molecule contains 3 atoms. That's about 5 sextillion atoms in one drop.

items.label: The Grapefruit Comparison
items.body: If you enlarged an atom to the size of a blueberry, then to keep the same scale, a blueberry would need to be enlarged to the size of the Earth.

items.label: Stars vs Atoms
items.body: There are an estimated 200 billion trillion stars in the observable universe. A single drop of water contains about 8 times more atoms than there are stars. Your glass of water outscores the night sky.

blocks.body: Despite being unimaginably tiny, atoms are not the smallest things that exist. They have internal structure — parts inside them that we'll explore next. But for chemistry, the atom is the fundamental unit. It's where elements get their identity.

blocks.title: Try to Visualize This
blocks.body: Hold out your hand and look at your palm. You're looking at roughly 7 octillion atoms — that's 7,000,000,000,000,000,000,000,000,000. Mostly carbon, oxygen, hydrogen, and nitrogen, arranged into molecules, arranged into proteins, arranged into cells, arranged into the skin you see.

Now pluck a single hair from your head (or imagine it). If you could zoom in on the cross-section of that hair, you'd find about one million atoms lined up across its width. A million. Across something you can barely see.

blocks.title: The Stardust in Your Hand
blocks.body: Every atom in your hand was forged inside a star. The carbon was created in a red giant. The iron was forged in a supernova. The oxygen was fused in a stellar core billions of years ago. Those atoms drifted through space, became part of a gas cloud, formed a planet, and eventually — you. You are literally holding stardust.

blocks.title: Count What You Can't See
blocks.body: Fill a glass with water and hold it up. In that glass are more atoms than grains of sand on every beach on Earth. More atoms than stars in the observable universe. And every single one is in constant motion — bumping, sliding, vibrating — even though the glass looks perfectly still to your eyes. The invisible world inside that glass is staggeringly busy.

blocks.title: Let's Talk About How Absurdly Small Atoms Are
blocks.body: People say atoms are small. That's like saying the ocean is damp. Let me give you some actual numbers that will ruin your sense of scale forever.

items.label: Hair Width
items.body: About one million atoms can line up across a single human hair. A MILLION. That hair you just pulled off your sweater is a HIGHWAY for atoms.

items.label: Drop of Water
items.body: One drop of water contains more atoms than there are stars in the entire observable universe. Let that sink in. A DROP. From your faucet. Outscoring the COSMOS.

items.label: The Blueberry Scale
items.body: If an atom were the size of a blueberry, a blueberry would need to be the size of Earth. So next time you eat a blueberry, know that it contains more atoms than there are blueberries needed to fill the planet. I think. The math gets weird.

items.label: Your Body
items.body: You contain approximately 7,000,000,000,000,000,000,000,000,000 atoms. That number has 27 zeros. Scientists call it 7 octillion. Normal people call it 'way too many zeros.'

blocks.body: Here's the existential kicker: every atom in your body was forged inside a dying star billions of years ago. The calcium in your bones came from a supernova explosion. The iron in your blood was cooked in a stellar core. You are literally dead star stuff walking around complaining about Mondays.

questions.question: About how many atoms can line up across the width of a single human hair?
questions.explanation: A human hair is about 100,000 nanometers wide. Atoms are about 0.1 to 0.3 nanometers across. That means roughly 1 million atoms could fit across the width of a single hair — showing just how incredibly tiny atoms are.

questions.question: A glass of water contains more atoms than there are stars in the observable universe. What does this tell you about atoms?

options.A: There aren't many stars in the universe
options.B: Water is special and contains more atoms than other substances
options.C: Atoms are so unimaginably tiny that even small amounts of matter contain astronomical numbers of them
options.D: Scientists have overestimated the number of atoms in water

questions.explanation: The comparison illustrates the extreme smallness of atoms. Because they're so tiny, even a small volume of any substance contains an incomprehensibly large number of them. This is true for all matter, not just water.

parts.purpose: Show how 118 elements build everything, introduce the next question (what's INSIDE an atom?), bridge to atomic_structure

blocks.title: The Universe's LEGO Set
blocks.body: Think about what we've established. There are only about 118 types of atoms in the entire universe. That's it. And from those 118 building blocks, nature constructs EVERYTHING.

items.label: Just 3 Elements → You
items.body: About 99% of your body (by atom count) is made from just three elements: hydrogen, oxygen, and carbon. Add nitrogen, calcium, and phosphorus and you're at 99.9%. You are astonishingly simple raw materials assembled in astonishingly complex ways.

items.label: Just 2 Elements → Water
items.body: Hydrogen (universe's lightest element) + oxygen (a reactive gas) = water (the liquid that makes life possible). Two atoms of one, one of the other. That's the recipe for the most important substance on Earth.

items.label: Just 1 Element → Diamond or Pencil
items.body: Pure carbon atoms arranged in a 3D grid = diamond, the hardest natural substance. The same carbon atoms arranged in flat sheets = graphite, soft enough to write with. Same atom, different arrangement, completely different material.

blocks.title: The Big Lesson
blocks.body: The type of atom determines the element. The arrangement of atoms determines the material. Chemistry is the study of how 118 types of atoms combine and arrange to create everything.

blocks.description: Infographic showing: 118 elements → combine into millions of compounds → build everything in the universe. Show a few familiar examples: H₂O, NaCl, CO₂, C₆H₁₂O₆

blocks.body: We know atoms exist. We know each element has a unique type of atom defined by its proton count. But what's actually INSIDE an atom? If protons determine the element, what else is in there? What makes atoms bond with each other? The answer requires us to crack open an atom and look inside — and what we find is mostly empty space, incredible density, and the key to all of chemistry: electrons. That's next.

blocks.title: See the Pattern Around You
blocks.body: Look around your room one more time with atom-eyes.

items.label: Find something that's mostly one element
items.hints: Aluminum foil (Al), copper wire in your charger (Cu), gold jewelry (Au), the graphite in a pencil (C)

items.label: Find something that's a compound of two elements
items.hints: Water (H + O), table salt (Na + Cl), rust on old metal (Fe + O)

items.label: Find something with carbon in it
items.hints: Anything alive or once alive — wood, paper, food, cotton, your own skin. Plus plastic, which is made from petroleum (ancient life). Carbon is EVERYWHERE in your daily life.

items.label: Find something with iron in it
items.hints: Steel tools, car keys, nails, paper clips. If it sticks to a magnet, it has iron atoms.

blocks.body: Every object you just found — from the aluminum foil to your own skin — is built from the same set of 118 atomic building blocks. The diversity you see comes from different combinations and arrangements of the same basic parts.

We've gone from 'everything is stuff' to 'everything is particles' to 'there are 118 types of particles called atoms.' The natural next question: what makes these atoms tick? What's INSIDE them that gives each element its unique personality? We're about to look inside the atom — and discover that these tiny building blocks have their own internal world.

blocks.title: The Universe Has 118 Ingredients
blocks.body: Here's what blows my mind. Every substance that has ever existed — every rock, every star, every taco, every weird deep-sea fish — is made from the same 118 types of atoms. That's like saying every meal ever cooked uses the same 118 ingredients. The universe is a kitchen with a very limited pantry but an infinitely creative chef.

items.label: You = 3 Main Ingredients
items.body: 99% of your atoms are hydrogen, oxygen, and carbon. That's it. You're basically a water-carbon combo with delusions of grandeur. Add nitrogen, calcium, and phosphorus and you've got 99.9% of a human.

items.label: Diamond vs Pencil = Same Ingredient
items.body: Both diamond and pencil graphite are pure carbon. Same atom. One is the hardest substance on Earth worth thousands of dollars. The other is so soft it writes on paper and costs 50 cents. Arrangement matters.

items.label: Salt = Two Dangerous Ingredients
items.body: Sodium (explodes in water) + chlorine (literal poison gas) = table salt (goes on fries). Chemistry's ultimate 'two wrongs make a right.'

blocks.body: Only about 30 of the 118 elements are important for everyday life. The rest are either super rare, radioactive, or were created in a lab for about 0.001 seconds before vanishing. Element 118 (oganesson) existed for less than a millisecond. Not exactly a team player.

So atoms are the building blocks of everything, and there are 118 types. But what's INSIDE an atom? If the number of protons determines the element, what ARE protons? What else is in there? Turns out, atoms have their own internal structure — and understanding it is the key to understanding why elements behave the way they do. Time to crack one open. Don't worry, no atoms will be harmed. Probably.

questions.question: Your body is 99% made of just three elements (by atom count). Which three?
questions.explanation: Hydrogen is the most abundant atom in your body (mostly in water), followed by oxygen (also in water, plus proteins), and carbon (the backbone of every organic molecule). These three elements account for about 99% of your atoms.
questions.tests_concept: elements_vs_compounds — element abundance in living things

questions.question: Diamond and graphite (pencil lead) are both made of pure carbon atoms. Why are they so different?

options.A: Diamond has more carbon atoms per gram than graphite
options.B: They're made of different types of carbon atoms
options.C: The atoms are the same but arranged in different structures — diamond in a 3D grid, graphite in flat sheets
options.D: Graphite has impurities mixed in that make it softer

questions.explanation: Same element, same atoms, but completely different arrangements. In diamond, each carbon bonds to 4 neighbors in a rigid 3D network. In graphite, carbons form flat sheets with weak connections between layers. Structure determines properties.
questions.tests_concept: elements_vs_compounds — atomic arrangement determines material properties

questions.question: There are about 118 known elements. How many are needed to make millions of different compounds?

options.A: All 118 are needed for each compound
options.B: Most compounds only need 2-6 of the 118 elements, combined in different ratios and arrangements
options.C: Only the first 10 elements can form compounds
options.D: Each compound requires its own unique element not shared with other compounds

questions.explanation: Most everyday compounds use a small handful of common elements (C, H, O, N, Na, Cl, Fe, Ca, etc.) combined in different ways. Water needs just 2 elements. Sugar needs 3. Your DNA needs 5. A small set of building blocks creates enormous variety through different combinations.
questions.tests_concept: elements_vs_compounds — combinatorial diversity from few elements
